content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814•2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Alan Smith 
City Attorney 
City of Watsonville 
Watsonville City Hall 
275 Main Street 
Watsonville, CA 95076 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-194 
Dear Mr. Smith: 
October 13, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions 
under Government Code Section 1090.1 Because the Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance (111 re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), this letter is based on the facts presented. 
Regarding our advice on Section l 090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Cruz County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (Section 1097.l(c)(5).) Please note that we are only 
providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of Section 1090 and not under other 
general conflict of interest prohibitions such as the Political Reform Act2 or common law conflict of 
interest. 
QUESTION 
Do the conflict of interest provisions of Section I 090 prohibit the police department from 
selling obsolete Taser devices to its officers? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Based upon the facts presented, the officers will not be making, participating in making, 
or influencing the sales contracts for purposes of Section 1090. 
1 All further statutory references arc to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. 
< The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. 


FACTS 
File No. A-15-194 
Page No. 2 
The city's police chief proposes to offer for sale to the department's officers 68 ten-year-old 
obsolete X26 Taser devices. The manufacturer of the devices has no buyback program and the cost 
of shipping the devices to other buyers would reduce their resale value. The officers would 
purchase the device for fair market value. 
ANALYSIS 
Section I 090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section I 090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall 11• City of Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section I 090 is intended "not only to 
strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial 
Beach 1•. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a 
financial interest. A contract that violates Section I 090 is void. The prohibition applies regardless 
of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Clltl ( 1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, pp. 646-649.) 
An official "makes" a contract if the official participates in any way in the making of the 
contract, including involvement in matters such as preliminary discussions, negotiations, planning. 
drawing of plans and specifications. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival 1•. City of 
Millbrae ( 1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall 11• City of Taft, supra at p. 569.) 
When an employee is financially interested in a contract, the employee's agency is 
prohibited from making the contract only if the employee participated in the contract-making 
process. Participation is defined very broadly. In Stigall, the court concluded that where a city 
councilmember had been involved in the preliminary stages of the planning and negotiating process 
on a city contract, but had resigned from the council prior to its vote on the contract, the 
council member had been involved in the making of the contract. (Stigall v. City of Taft, supra. 58 
Cal.2d. at pp. 570-571.) The Stigall court noted that "negotiations, discussions, reasoning, planning 
and give and take which goes beforehand in the making of the decision to commit oneself must all 
be deemed to be a part of the making of an agreement in the broad sense." (Id. at p. 569.) Therefore, 
any participation by a financially interested officer or employee in the process by which such a 
contract is developed, negotiated, and executed is a violation of section 1090. 
As long as the employee plays no role whatsoever in the contracting process (either because 
such participation is outside the scope of the employee's duties or because the employee 
disqualifies himself or herself from all such participation), the employee's agency is not prohibited 
from contracting with the employee. (See 80 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 41 (1997) [firefighters permitted to 
sell a product, which they invented in their private capacity, to their fire department so long as they 
did not participate in the sale in their official capacity]; 63 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 868 (1980) [real 
estate tax appraiser could purchase property within the county at a tax-deeded land sale where he 
did not participate in or influence the appraisal].) 


File No. A-15-194 
Page No. 3 
According to the facts you have provided, the police officers have had no input in the police 
chiefs decision-making process at any stage. Therefore, the officers are not "making" a contract for 
the purposes of Section l 090 and the department may offer the Taser devices to the officers for 
sale. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

selling obsolete Taser devices to its officers?

< The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014.

VJ:jgl

selling obsolete Taser devices to its officers?

CONCLUSION: No. Based upon the facts presented, the officers will not be making, participating in making, 
or influencing the sales contracts for purposes of Section 1090.

< The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014.
